CURRENT AFFAIRS.

It is one of our greatest consolations that many of
those who bitterly haté us have to keep on reading us;
‘that is, indeed, a tribute of which we are justly proud.

_ Last weel we received a most complimentary anony-
mous letter bearing the postmark ‘‘ Kingstown.’
inonymous Kingstown correspondent is, indeed, & flat-
terer he hates us, yet we are that fascinating that he

u iue to be & reader. ~ Our Kingstown anony-

i

Our

nces hishostile effusion-this

““Dear Mr. Epiror,—If you are consistent—
and I have great doubts latterly—the LEADER, next
week, will ring with angry denunciations of Red-
mond, Dillon and Devlin for publicly desecrating &
solemn Fast Day of the Church in their anxiety not
to harass the members of the Eighty Club. It
may be that you prefer to reserve all your ire for
the Past Clongownian, who (made famous by your
critical pen) eat roast turkey on a certain other
occasion.

Further on he says:—

‘* Likely the fear of losing the weekly list of sub-
scriptions to the Parliamentary Fund will make
you cautious—and silent. If you ignore this
flagrant breach of Ecclesiastical Law by the above
accommodating trio, your conduct, like theirs, is
contemptible, and will merit the sneers of all right-
minded Catholics and public men—in the, purity of
whose lives you have apparently been so interested
in the past.”

TFor all we know to the contrary this anonymous
correspondent may be Capt. Wade Thompson, the Per-
vert Priest Protector, or it may be Brother Goulding.
What flatters us is that, whoever he is, he hates us
and yet he says he has read every number of the
LEADER.

Some political club in England had a dinner on 2
fast day—there is nothing extraordinary about that as
Tngland is a Protestant country. = Messrs. Redmond,
Dillon and Devlin went to the dinner, but how did they
break ecclesiastical law by doing so? = After all we all
dine on fast days; it is not the fact of dining that is
forbidden to Catholics, it is the eating of meat. There
is no evidence that Messrs. Redmond, Dillon and
Devlin ate meat at the dinner—we are quite satisfied
that they did not—and so there is nothing against them.
It was rather tantalising for them to see their English
““saved "’ friends tucking in whilst they had to decline
everything but the fish.  Perhaps if, which we doubt,
our anonymous correspondent is a professing ‘‘ idola-
ter,” he may, be a weakling who could not trust himself
before roast duck on a fast day. The Past Clon-

' gownian whom we made, shall we say, famous, boasted
that he ate roast turkey on a fast day; there is no
evidence that Redmond, Dillon and Devlin did any-
thing of the sort, and if we denounced them for doing
what we believe they did not do, we would be doing
an injustice, and incidentally might be brought before
the courts for libel. ~ Our anonymous correspondent:
would not shed tears over the latter, we suppose. Our
anonymous correspondent had better keep reading on

. week by week, and he may trip us up in the end—

you never can tell. He hai missed fire this time, bub
we thank him for the comvliment he pays the LEADER
by reading it every week, though he hates i. i

A STORY FOR BOYS—AND MEN.

Bill Sturdy was a youngster queer—
In fact he was no class; :

His father was an engineer
Connected with the gas.

The engineer, although ﬁb fool,
Once showed his wits were lamed
ByF sending Jim unto a school
1 : ull

Poor Jim among the scions grand,
Of haughty huckster race,

Felt like a fish upon the land,
And long disliked the place.

For Cricket, Rugby and the games,
That suit young Bung and Pawn,

A wholesome deep contempt had James
Who loved the old caman.

But that no scholar there did wield ;
It smacked of Ireland low.

All games upon the sporting field
Were English, don’t you know.

Soon when the boys perceived that Jim
Was all for fashions old,

They started playing tricks on him
Most impudent and bold.

They jeered him as a rustic clown
Until with anger high

He knocked a rising bank clerk down,
With one upon the eye.

Then at him came a youthful gent
Intended for the law,

But down to grass he sprawling went
With left hook on the jaw.

In one short day before his hand
Three budding doctors fell,

Two sprouting civil servants and
A rising army swell.

This changed the programme in a trice;
To Jim, the rustic lout,

The lads at once grew very nice
For they did fear a clout.

But Jim, I fear, grew very rude
And often made remarks

Most hurtful to the cultured brood
Of barristers and clerks.

And when his teacher to him spoke
About his future toil,

Jim said he meant to work in smoke,
Machinery and oil.

At this the teacher sore surprised
Away from him did pass.

He loved a bank clerk, but despised
An engineer of gas.

But Jim, he didn’t care a groat
For any cultured snob,

And leaving school he doffed -his coat
And started at his job.

THE LEADER.

MarcH 16, 1912.

And now a mighty man of gas
Is this unruly James;

A terror to all sham and class,
To bigots and to tames. A M. W.
An original suggestion was made by Father James

Hughes, in the course of a lecture on “ The New ].re-

land,” in the Arts Theatre of the Liverpool University.

In the course of his references to the influence of the

Trish priests, he remarked, according to a Press report:

“He would like to see each Irish parish broken
up into two or more parishes, for bhen'men, in-
stead of becoming parish priests at over sixty years
of age, would take on the responsibility at about
forty, when the best years of life were before them,
and when, though they had gathered a certain
amount of experience and wisdom, they had not
lost all their youthful enthusiasm and energy. That
would do more to build up the social well-being of
the Irish people than-any legislative measure that
ever had passed, or ever would pass, the House of
Commons.”’

We are sure that the curates will view that suggestion
with sympathy.

The Fintan Lalor Branch of the Gaelic League,
Mountmellick, ask us to call attention for the informa-
tion of our readers in the district to the fact that they
have opened a class for the teaching of ITrish dancing, to
be conducted on Saturdays from 12 noon to 2 p.m., in
the Owenass Hall. Mr. Michl. Dunne, prize medallist,
is the teacher. Trish Language classes are held on
every Tuesday night in the same hall.

a few words on ‘ Home Rule and Bung.” I hold no

and feel full practical sympathy with the strenuous

also the numbers of the casks, must be entered in ‘all

official books and documents.” - No further marks or"

\\'a,e have received a copy of ‘“ Guy’s Limerick Direc-
tory ** for 1912. One is accustomed to associate direc-
tories of all kinds with stiff prices, but this Limerick
directory is only 6d. It contains a list of the house-
holders of Limerick City and suburbs, a business direc-
tory of the city, and particulars of various institu-
tions in the city. Under the heading * City Churches ”
the old Ascendancy spirit displays itself, for the so-
called ““ Church of Ireland,’” which even legally is dis-
established, and which in reality is at best the Church of
England’s Faithful Garrison in Ireland, is given pride
of place. Even Baptist, Presbyterian and Methodist
C:lmpels are given precedence of the Catholic Churches.
The Protestant Bishop is placed before the Catholic
Bishop. Surely at this time of day these matters
should be placed in their proper perspective in a Lime-
rick directory. The directory is very well turned out,
and it is very cheap at sixpence.

The Bishop of Cloyne’s action against the ‘“ Dundee
Courier ”’ naturally reminds us of the foul libel com-
mitted by the ‘‘Irish Times’’ against Canon McInerney.
The * Dundee Courier *’ has to pay £200 to his Lord-
ship and £50 each to six Queenstown priests—£500 in
all, and we suppose the costs will be at least £1,000,
so that at a moderate computation the article in the
issue of August 15th, headed ‘‘ Sinister Sidelights on
Home Rule.—Irish Incident Showing Feeling Towards
Britain.—By One Who Has Lived in Ireland,” must
cost the * Dundee Courier "’ £1,500. It may be more
canny next time.

General libels are cheap. The ““ Irish Times’’ had
an infamous libel on the Nationalists and Catholics of
Dundalk a few days ago in the form of an anonymous
letter.  That libel was denounced in the local paper,
the “ Dundalk Democrat,”’ over his own name by a
Scotch. non-Catholic settled in Dundalk, as a conse-
quence of our Dundalk contemporary quoting our notes
about it.  But up to this day we have not seen any
protest against it in the *‘ Irish Times,”’ nor have we
seen any apology in the ‘‘ Irish Times ' for its gross
libel.  The libel was so general that it might be im-
possible to get at the “ Irish Times,”’ as happily some
‘years ago Canon Mclnerney was able to get at it.

[t is “now about eight years ago since the ‘‘ Irish
Times,’’ then edited by a man named Locler, let itself
in for trouble by libelling Canon McInerney.  The
libellous letter was sent by a self-styled “‘Roman Catho-
lic Farmer,” and purported to come from Bridgetown,
Killaloe. The yile letter found welconie in the pages
of the ‘“ Irish Times,”’ which was then, we.-believe,
even more bitterly anti-Catholic and anti-Trish than it
now is. The ‘‘ Irish Times * atterapted to stand over

~ its bogus letter and claimed it as fair comment. The

editor of the ““Irish Times *’ sent two registered letters
to the address given by its bogus “ Roman Catholic
Farmer,”” but as the address was bogus, théy did not

an anonymous liar who was accorded hospitality in-the
““ Trish Times,’’ sought for the name of the libeller, the

““ Irish Times '’ retorted :—'‘ Father McInerney is mis- -

taken as to the practice of respectable journals in regard
to correspondents. There never was a time when a
respectable journal would not give shelter to the letter

of correspondents who had reason to believe that the -

publication of their names might lead to such persecu-
tion as that which Father McInerney not obscurely
threatens.””  But Canon McInerney was no white-
livered ‘‘ tame,” and in the end the ‘Irish Times 2
dropped on its dishonoured knees, cried peccavi, and
paid £450, When Canon MecInerney’s examination was
finished the ‘ Irish Times’ '’ counsel said there were
reasons why he should not cross-examine till the next
morning—as it so happened there were reasons why
the ‘“ Irish Times’ *’ counsel would never cross-examine.
In the morning the knees of the discredited, old, anti-
Catholic bigot, the:‘‘ Irish Times,” were knocking to-
gether, and in sack-cloth and ashes the filthy libellers
came into court with an abject apology in one hand
and £450 in-the other. The ‘‘ Irish Times '’ was beaten
and humiliated.

The libeller in the ““ Dundee Couricr’” was fairly
general, but he was not sufficiently so. In the course
of the article he said:—

‘“ Two years ago, in Queenstown, Co. Cork, in-
structions- were issued by the Roman Catholic re--
ligious authorities that all Protestant shop assis-
tants were to be discharged. One shopkeeper, &
Roman Catholic, refused to discharge an assistant
he had had for a number of years. The consequence
was that his shop was proclaimed, and in three
months he had to close and clear out, his stock
being sold for next to nothing. He and his family
left for Britain, where, as he said, he would employ
an atheist if he liked.”

The Scotch jury found unanimously for his Lordship
the Bishop of Cloyne and for the six priests, and the
damages, as we have already said, were £200 for the
Bishop and £50 each for the priests. The  Irish
Times’’ gave the second day’s hearing of the case in its
Monday issue, about half-a-column, stowed away on
page 9, whilst the report in the *“ Freeman’’ of the first
day ran to nearly seven columns, and of the second day
to nearly four columns. Almost needless to say, the
““Trish Times’”had not an editorial word about it; we
suppose it was thinking sadly of its libel on Canon
McInerney.

The anonymous letter against Dundalk Catholics and
Nationalists was, perhaps, as foul and low a libel as
the libel of the ‘“ Dundee, Courier’' against his Lord-
ship Dr. Browne and the six priests. But in the case
of the *‘ Irish Times’ * libel on Dundalk, it may be im-
possible to bring it to court; it is none the less, but
rather the more, dastardly for that.

A Dundalk bungery, run by a Mrs. Courtney, Park
Street, was advanced upon from the front and rear by
two constables, and several bogus *‘ bonas’’ were neatly
caught. Mrs. Courtney had been fined 20s. no later
than December 8th of last year. The solicitor for the
bungery appealed to the magistrates’ clemency; his
client was guilty of & breach of the law, but it was very
hard for publicans to live in these days of keen com-
petition-and oppressive legislation| The poor bungeries

What strange and varied uses is St. Patrick’s Day
made to serve? We know the base use of which the
Bungs, or at least a large section of them, made of St.
Patrick’s Day. Real national sentiment having made
a secular holiday of St. Patrick’s Day, a lot of the
Bungs hastened to use the new opportunity and pro-
ceeded to sponge on_ St. Patrick. We see by a Drog-
heda paper that the Leinster Regiment of ‘‘ Tommies,”’
stationed at Millmount Barracks, are to turn out next
“St. Patrick’s Day in full dress uniform, ** with their
new band of Irish pipers, which the regiment has re-
cently acquired, thanks to the interest evinced in the
movement by Capt. Orpen-Palmer, whe has bad the
co-operation of such an authority on ancient Celtic
pipes as Lord Castletown.”” The note in the local
paper also informs us that ‘‘ the new pipes are an
exact replica of the old Irish war pipes, and will play
very fine march music.”’ So Tommy Atkins is exploit-
ing the Irish pipes! It is a wonder that Tommy does
not go in for playing hwling! And for learning
O’Growney !

There is a scheme on foot to do something handsome
in the way of pier and baths at Merrion by the Pem-
broke U.D.C.  The idea is one that will commend
itsell to residents of the Pembroke Township and be-
yond it, and it is plain that such a scheme if satisfac-
torily carried out would add greatly to the residential
attractions of a large part of the township and neigh-
bouring districts. ~We have often thought that a
stranger coming to Dublin must wonder at the lack of
anything like modern development of Merrion. We
know that Sandymount-on-Sea has become a cheap
joke, and it requires pluck to talk of the beauty of
Merrion. Yet Merrion has great natural advantages,
and its proximity to the’ city cries aloud that its possi-
bilities for bathing and swimming should at least be
developed. We hope the local Council will carry out a
satisfactory schente.

CATHOLICS AND THE INSURANCE ACT.

l DESIRE to draw the attention of Catholics, more

especially clergymen, to the necessity of imme-
diate action in connection with the Insurance  Act.
It seems to me very strange that, notwithstanding all
the papers, magazines, and books that have appeaved
on this subject, many people are still -unacquainted
with its provisions. . It is a measure that lends itself
to much misrepresentation, because it is easier to criti-
cise than to put forward a better scheme than that pro-
posed. The ‘Act requires all persons engaged in manual
labour, irrespective of income and those employed in
non-manual labour with incomes below £160, to be
compulsory insured with certain exceptions. The onus
is put on the employer, under a penalty of £10, to pay
his own contribution and that of the employed, and
deduct the latter’s share from his wages. Payment is
made by means of stamps on a card to be held by the
employed person.  This card will not show to what

employees working under Protestant employers. Here
I may remark that employers have no right under the
Act to interfere with their employees in respect of what
society the latter joins. I have reason to believe that
some large employers in this city are using compulsion
on their workers to make them join the Irish Health
Society; the workers cannot be blamed for giving in
if the only alternative is to lose their positions.

Members of approved societies are the favourites of
fortune under the Act. If a person does not join an
approved society, he or she will have to become insured
through the Post Office ; from the amount to their credit
(including employers, their own and the State grant)
ab the beginning of each year will be deducted a contri-
bution (1) for sanatoria, (2) for administration ; and they
can draw on the rest as long as it lasts. Members of
approved societies, on the other hand, after six months’
membership, are entitled to 10s. a week for 26 weeks
for men, and 7s. 6d. for women for the same period,
sanatorium and maternity benefits, and after two years’
membership to 5s. a week till 70 if permanently dis-
abled.

The Act is to be worked through approved societies
and local Health Committees. It is to these bodies I
desire to draw attention, and for weighty reasons. An
approved society under the Insurance Act needs no
fixed amount of members; but as there will be a valua-
tion of assets and liabilities of each society every three
years, a society if it be not 5,000 members will be
grouped with another so as to make up that number.

Approved societies have to fulfil certain conditions—
(1) They must keep books, ete., to be audited, (2) money
| acquired under this Act can be used for no other
I purpose, (3) security will have to be given by the
officers of such societies unless the societies can pay
benefits out of their own funds and look to the Commis-
sioners for recoupment, (4) they cannot refuse an appli-
cation of membership merely on the ground of age,
(5) there must be absolute self-government ; the election
of the committee must be by ballot where possible.
[ These are the most important; perhaps, I may add their
i rules will have to be sanctioned by the Insurance Com-
missioners, as also their place of meeting.  Special
arrangements are made whereby employers’ provident
societies can become approved; the election of com-
mittee other than those the employer nominates must
be by ballot; the employer must guarantee
their solvency in addition to paying his contributions
under the Act. There is no reason why various bodies
of Catholics, particularly sodalities, should not com-
bine to form an approved society. Steps ought to be
taken by the clergy in this matter. The larger the
membership the better, because the risks will be wide-
i spread. The various Protestant -churches™ are out
. organising approved societies for all they are worth.

Catholics shonld band together, as this Act gives a great

chance of their becoming organised; the great majority

of the insured will be Catholics; any positions to be
filled in connection with such societies will go to Catho-
lics. A move has been made in the united parishes,

The position of the Insurance or Health Committees
has been greatly extended from what at first was in-
tended. Originally formed to act as an approved
society for deposit contributors, they now have also
charge of the sanatorium benefit. In each county an
Tnsurance Committee will be set up, consisting of 24
members ; 12 elected by the members of approved socie-
ties and the deposit contributors; 8 nominated by the
County Council; 4 nominated by the Insurance Com-
missioners. It will be seen that the msured members
form one-half of each committee; they will be selected
by the various approved societies and deposit contri-
butors in proportion to their numbers. If there are
Catholic societies with a large membership what a
chance of getting a working majority on these com-
mittees!

These Health Committees can apply to the Local
Government Board wherever excessive sickness pre-
vails, to hold an inquiry into the causes of such visita-
tion; if the fault be due to a local Council failing to
carry out the powers at its disposal, such body will
have to pay extra cost of such sickness. The Health
Committees can publish reports on, and give lectures re,
health; and have the assistance of the local Medical
Officer of Health if permitted by his Council to attend.
They can allow certificates of exemption from compul-
sory insurance if the Commissioners so empower them;
can state what extra benefits are to be given where
the sickness or disablement benefit is curtailed because
it exceeds two-thirds worker's wages ; they can value the
weekly allowance of a lump sum worker has got for
compensation re accidents. Health Committees are
also to administer maternity benefits to persons not
members of approved societies (other than deposit con-
tributors). = They can apply to the County Councils,
with the sanction of the Treasury, to raise a rate to be
devoted to the extension of their work; half such extra
rate to be raised from the county, half to be given by the
Treasury. Moreover, these committees are empowered
to appoint District Committees, to whom they can dele-
gate all their powers for the said district. I have ex-
plained the powers and composition of these bodies at
some length. I desire to point out that Cathelics
must, in the interests of their poorer co-religionists who
form the great majority of insured persons, organise and
give no further opportunities to the ““ saved ’’ of prosel-
tysing. Of course, I am prepared to give my Protes-
tant countrymen a better representation on these com-

LOUVAIN UNIVERSITY.

HAVE before me the Annuaire, or Calender of

Louvain University for 1912, and a glance through

it reveals once more the striking progress and continued
success of that great Catholic Institution,

On the outside page I find the likeness of Pope
Martin V., who founded the old and famous University
in the year 1425. On the first page inside, what do I
find? I find a Prayer to The Holy Mother of God, the
Patroness of the University. I make no apology to the
““ non-sectarians,” or secularists, for noting these two
proofs of Catholic Faith, namely, Devotiou to the Mother
of the Divine Redeemer and loyalty to Christ’s Vicar on
earth. Catholics have as good a right to place their
Universities under the patronage of their heavenly and
earthly patrons as the Protestant Trinity College and
the Presbyterian Belfast College have to place their
institutions under the religious patrons of their choice.

The Catholics of Belgium, at least, are neither a raid
nor ashamed to make profession of their Faith—and
what is even better, to practice that Faith in their great
University. They built up their Unmversity by their
own energy, perseverance, and unity, under the able,
zealous and vigilant guidance of their national Hier-
archy. The present head of that Hierarchy is Card inal
Mercier, Primate of Belgium, who, it may be recalled,
travelled to London in company with M. Nerinex, a
Professor of Law in Louvain, and gave most valuable
evidence before the Commission on Irish University
Education in 1901. He is now a tower of strength to
the Louvain University, and it is most likely that he
was instrumental in obtaining from the Government a
measure passed last year giving to Louvain (and also
to Brussels) University a legal corporate status, which
it had not previously possessed.

There are now 150 professors of all kinds in the
various Faculties of Louvain University =~ The work of
the University is carried on in thirty-seven different
Colleges and Academical Institutes, all fully equipped
for the necessary work of the various departments.
The number of students last year reached the grand
fotal of 2,630. I find there are no female students in
Tiouvain University. The following Table shows the
continued progress in the number of students since 1834,
the year the University was re-established by the Bel-

ducted by the students. Last year, out of the 14 Travel-
ling Burses offered by the Government for competition
amongst the four Belgian Universities (Liege and
Ghent are well-endowed Government Universities),

competition for two engineers to supervise State rail-
ways, two Louvain engineers were ‘placed first and

THE GENTLE ART OF CONVERSE.

TIVHE art of Conversation, lile that of Polite Letter-

writing, is fast falling into abeyance. The reason
is not far to seek.  Conversation has declined at the
same time, though not from the same cause as letter-
writing.  There was a time when newspapers were

scarce, telegrams and telephones did not exist, and.the -

chief medium for the spread of intelligence lay in
letters. Nowadays, however, if one desires information
on any topies, save those of a purely private and domes-
tic nature, he does not look to the long-winded, closely-
written pages of & gossipy friend. A picture postcard,
with scribbled initials, is the limit of correspondence
from those who travel. At one time the traveller wrote
long letters home, of which he usually kept copies, and

letters into a dreary book. Events at home, also,
pohhcp.l or social, were not in those days recorded—
sometimes before they have happened—by a syndicated
Press, and hence a leter from a friend was highly
pr)zed_ . But the agencies for the diffusion of knowledge
to which I have referred have put a stop to that. Nor
does one regret it. But the decline of Conversation is
quite another matter, and one which must surely make
the judicious grieve.

So far has Conversation, in any proper sense, ceased
to exist that, save for the purpose of proclaiming their
wants, most human beings might as well be
born dumb. The devastating influence of Bridge
is held by some as accountable. Yet it is not
rightly to be blamed. People have not ceased
talking because they desire to play Bridge;
they play Bridge because they are not any longer able
to talk. The student of the habits and customs of
former days will recall that an age addicted to gambling,
and to cards-in particular, was in a special degree an
age of Conversation. Men were as proud of their
prowess in Talk in those days as they now are of their
scor¢ at Bridge or their handicap at Golf. That it was
a more intellectual form of triumph will scarcely be
denied.  Yet the wits were far from being averse to
the dice or the card table. = The golden age of the
gamester stands out also as the golden age of the talker.
Silence is more usually associated with the shining
adjective, but not wisely so. Conversation speech
may sparkle and glitter, but it is impossible for a thing
that does not exist to dazzle by its brilliance.

Af the same time it is too often forgotten that true
Conversation depends as much on one’s power for
silence as on one’s power to talk. The neglect of
this obvious and fundamental truth is, perhaps, one of
the contributory causes of the decay of the art of con-
verse. Too many people are, or should we not rather
say were? inclined to do all the talking themselves.
‘This is natural enough since it is only human to delight
in the sound of one’s voice. The sound of another’s is
less charming. Only by keeping this fact well in mind
may Conversation be promoted. ~Remember, even in
the act of uttering your most brilliant phrase, your
most popular mot, that the delight of your auditors is,
in part, feigned. Each is simply pining to cap your
wit by his own, to outshine your sparkle, to scintillate
in his turn. A game at talking is, in some respects, like
a game ‘at tennis. The players must all have a share
if they are all to enjoy it.

I have said that Bridge has not killed Talk; it has
merely replaced it.  Assemble a number of people to-
gether in a drawingroom. How long will it be before
they yawn unless you make them form fours with
painted paste-boards in their hands, silent, serious, aye
solemn, round small, green tables? Not a word is then
heard, save now and again such mystic phrases as: o],
leave it to you, partner,” or “I'll go no trumps.”’
Groans and curses, not loud, but deep, occasionally
break on the ear. The rest is silence.

Now I am not to be taken as an out-and-out opponent
of Cards or even Bridge. True, I would rather endure
the dreaviest depths of deadly boredom than sit for ten

minutes at a card-table. I would rather play Bil-
liards, even—it I knew how. But, though a total ab=
stainer from Cards, I'm not a fanatic. I would nob seek,
like so many teetotallers in another connection, to pre-
vent the enjoyment of others. Cards may be played,
surely, from time to time as a relaxation, _but to con-
stitute them the sole indoor form of som;\l intercourse,
is in my judgment to reduce to a nullity the gift of
speech by which we are distinguished from the beasts
that perish. Surely our tongues were not given to us
solely to enable us to eat! Some months ago the sage,
“ Chanel,’’ gave his views on this matter to the readers
of this paper. But ‘ Chanel” is a teetotaller in the
restricted sense.  Articles of his in the I h'ave
disclosed an almost violent bias on the subject of Drink.

And as is only to be expected from the dl'sclples.of
Temperance, ‘‘ Chanel "’ has condemned Bridge with
lack of moderation, such as teetotallers are notorious
for. I, as I have said, am no rigid Total Abstainer.
For the good of my health, the gladness of my heart,
and even on occasion the dissipation of my thirst, I now
and again quaff of ‘‘the cup that clears to-day of past
regrets and future fear.”” Therefore, I would impose
no tax on the Bridge-player. I would not compel him
to finish his game at a fixed hour. I would not say of
him to another, in hushed and mournful tonmes: ‘A
decent fellow, but he plays.”

My attack on Bridge in its present influence on
society, and on all games of a like character, is, there-
fore, not because they are inherently wrong, or because
they have by their superior attractions replaced Con-
versation, but simply because the auxiliary has been
allowed to oust the main attraction. People do nob
tallk nowadays merely because they are too lazy and
too selfish to do so. Instead of expressing his views on
affairs of moment, abstract philosophies, or ultimate
things in language, grave, paradoxical, serious or gay,
the modern man is content to mutter a few words of
unsanctioned slang. He says as little as he can, as
badly as he can. Naturally such interchanges of words
do not prove interesting, and no wonder that, after half-
an-hour of this desultory chat, even the card-table
comes as g relief. Nor is it modern inventions that
have stifled Conversation as they have slain letter-
writing.  There is vastly more to talk about—and to
talk well about—now thian there was a hundred years
ago.  Thé old-time jousts of talking have ceased be-
cause men have lost heart for the tourney. The
modern man does not enjoy the combat of words. A
dull level of agreement, a drab commonplace of notions
is much more to his liking:

Everyone—if paralysis of the tongue and of the mind
is to be avoided—should be made to talk, and moreover
to talk in an accepted language—not in slang. Thus
only can the excesses of the card-table be mitigated and
men escape becoming in reality dumb, Bridge-driven
cattle, if we may paraphrase the poet’s line.

may be either short or long as the competitor pleases.
A good short story will be preferred to a bad long one;
a good long story will be preferable to a good short

tains the reader. ~
; Let me give a few instances of what I mean.
‘:\slt-my in the Forest,”’ by 1. S. Ellis, is a most inte-
resting, thrilling, tale of boy peril, in which no human
life is recklessly destroyed, as it is in so many tales of
Indian adventure. Again, here are two excellent plots
of school stories which appeared in the Messenger of
the Sacred Heart by an author too modest to sign his
name. One is entitled ‘“ The True Boy,”’ the other,
“ The New Boy.” In one case the hero creeps by
night to the oratory to say a prayer for his mother—it
is the anniversary of her death; the sneak is on the
corridor for a different purpose—to have an illicit
smoke. The prefect catches our hero, who puts up
with a flogging, the loss of his good name, and dis-
missal from the Sodality, a great sacrifice to him,
rather than betray the true culprit. In the second
story the hero finds himself at a Protestant school.
On Friday meat is served and he is jeered for not eat-
ing it. One Friday the bully of the school is particu-
larly offensive, setting a dish of soup before our hero
with orders that there is to be ‘‘no bigotry " there,

between the two eyes of his tormentor, and cast the
hot. soup over him. There is a moment’s consterna-
tion; then the ‘‘ true ”’ boy is cheered, chaired, and
elected captain of games. These synopses do not, of
course, do justice to the stories, but they suggest the
plot, excellent in both cases.

fanity to keep the matter from the authorities. Bub
the Talf-crown bribe has scarcely sunk to the bottom of

Let me now, even ab the cost of being considered- a
bit egotistical, cite an incident that ocourred since my
last article: for it seems to me as if the finger of God
is in the timely revelation. About a year ago or less
I found once or twice a boy of 15 reading under the
gas jet in the street the class of literature I am dealing
with, at about 11 o’clock. I advised him to go home,
but not having read the papers, and so being under the
impression that they were harmless, I told him to read
them at another time. He told his parents that I ap-
proved of his reading, which thus went on unchecked.
He soon went a.bit wrong, organised some of the boys
of the district into a troop of Apaches, whose doings
were, I fear, more than mere boyish mischief. He
left the parish and I lost sight of him. Last week,
however, his mother came to me and asked me to call
and give him some advice: for he was staying ouf at
night and mitching from school. She then showed me
a book she had discovered in his possession. I found
it to be a French publication of the vilest kind. Lust
iz its theme from cover to cover, while as to the illus-
trations—it is no figure of speech, it is literally a fact
—nothing could be worse.

The boy is not, of course, to be taken as a type of
our lads in general: he is an exception undoubtedly.
But I suggest that this final stage of the boy’s depra-
vity was reached through the low literature with which
so many of our boys are growing more and more fami-
liar and which is warping their moral sense.

If this is so, the question may be put without hesi-
tation to parents, to heads of schools and colleges, to
every honest man who has at heart the interest of his
country in the rising generation : have they not a clear
duty in this matter? - Can anything be done ?

(Rev?) J. M. CosTELLO.

Some object to Mr. Ward for having given us so
fully Newman's account. of the failure of his attempt
to start a Catholic University in Ireland on the lines
of that of Louvain. On the other hand, I think
it would be well if Mr. Ward were to publish in a cheap
form the chapters on Ireland, as the book itself, cost-
ing 86s., is out of the reach of many Irishmen who
would like to read Newman’s own account of this

' failure.  Leo XIII. recommended people in writing
history to be truthful and not to hide facts. We seem
sometimes to forget that God Is truth, and that truth
can never injure God or His Church. I think, there-
fore, it would be very useful for Irishmen to know
Newman’s view of why this attempt to establish a
Catholic University in Ireland was such a dismal failure.

Newman threw himself with all his energy into the
work, although it was contrary to his own judgment, in
obedience to what he thought was the wish of Pius IX.
The Irish Bishops, who were in authority, wanted New-
man’s name, but did not trust him, and, therefore, did
not give him a free hand. Many of them were kind
and civil to him, but Dr. Cullen, who was practically
supreme, not only did not trust him, but did not even
answer his letters. People may differ as to who was
right at the time—Cardinal Newman or Cardinal Cullen
—but Leo XIII., during his Pontificate, seems after-
wards, by making Newman a Cardinal, to have sup-
ported the views of Newman rather than those of
Cullen, Manning, Ward, and Talbot, and we know that
Leo XIII. was one of the greatest Popes the world has
seen.

In 1851, in a letter to Mrs. William Froude, Newman
wrif ““ I suppose in a few days I shall know what is
decided on in Ireland about the University. It is a
most daring attempt, but first it is a religious one,
next it has the Pope’s blessing on it. Curious it will be
if Oxford is imported into Ireland, not in its members
only, but in ifs principles, methods, ways, and argu-
ments. The battle there will be what it was in Oxford
University twenty years ago. Curious, too, . . . . .
that while I found my tools breaking under me in
Oxford, for Protestantism is not susceptible of so high
a temper, I am renewing the struggle in Dublin with
the Catholic Church to support me. It is very wonder-
ful—Keble, Pusey, Maurice, Sewell, etc., who have
been able to do so little agamst Liberalism in Oxford,
will be renewing the fight, although not in their per-
sons, in Ireland.”
